articles.summary: Struggling to focus? Hubstaff [4] looked at how the 140,000 people get work done. Turns out:

articles.title: THE FUTURE OF AI CREATIVE TOOLS
articles.summary: FLORA, an AI-powered creative tool company, raised $42 million in Series A funding to give professional creatives more control over their workflows. Founder Weber Wong criticized Silicon Valley for misunderstanding creatives' needs, emphasizing the importance of taste and control in creative work. The platform's node-based interface supports companies like Nike and Lionsgate by integrating generative models into customizable workflows, enabling both manual and autonomous creation.

articles.title: TRENDS I'M SEEING IN AI PRICING
articles.summary: AI pricing is getting harder as agents are running longer and more autonomously. People dislike usage-based billing because it is unpredictable, and per-seat pricing doesn't map cleanly to AI value. One pricing model that has prevailed in 'credit', where customers pre-purchase tokens for work. Credits will likely stick around, but they still don't make AI spend feel predictable, as agents can do wildly unpredictable amounts of work. AI products prioritizing billing observability would make AI feel more like software and less like a casino.

articles.title: ENTIRE INDUSTRIES ARE NOW DECIDED IN <2 YEARS
articles.summary: Becoming the industry default used to take 20 years. That's how long it took for "no one gets fired for buying IBM" to become true. With AI, that timeline is under two. Harvey signed A&O Shearman when it was four people, and that one deal made it credible enough to become the default in legal AI. Landing a big customer and building industry credibility aren't separate phases - they feed each other. Most startups treat them as sequential. The ones who lock in the default do both at once.

articles.title: MY 3 CRITERIA FOR SUCCESS IN EARLY-STAGE COMPANIES
articles.summary: Netflix co-founder Marc Randolph argues that the single biggest determinant of early-stage startup success is the team, emphasizing execution, adaptability, and persistence over product or funding. He explains that talent operates on a power curve and shares practical advice on attracting and retaining exceptional people, including hiring for future impact, cutting toxic high performers, and treating employees with trust and honesty.

articles.title: MANAGEMENT AS AI SUPERPOWER
articles.summary: An experimental class at the University of Pennsylvania demonstrated how management and AI tools like Claude Code and ChatGPT can accelerate startup development. Students rapidly created diverse prototypes by leveraging AI for market research, financial modeling, and ideation, effectively cutting down traditional startup timelines. Successful delegation to AI hinges on balancing task complexity, AI success probability, and evaluation time, emphasizing the value of management skills in optimizing AI productivity.

articles.title: REACH REAL BUYERS IN TLDR INSTEAD OF FIGHTING FOR ATTENTION ON PAID SOCIAL
articles.summary: TLDR puts you in front of your target audience with almost no competition. Each issue features only three advertisers max, giving your message the space it needs to stand out. Millions of developers, product managers, CEOs, and other tech decision makers actually see what you have to say. Learn more [10] about running a test campaign.

articles.summary: AI-powered Q&A for GitHub repositories that clones and analyzes codebases to deliver source-backed answers to developer questions.

articles.summary: 12 months of free Eleven Labs credits if you are an early-stage startup.

articles.title: IS YOUR MARGIN MY OPPORTUNITY IN SOFTWARE?
articles.summary: AWS's operating margins ranged between 25% to 38% between 2015 and 2025. The company prints money at scale even after a decade of cloud commoditization. Its moat is capex, and few other companies have the balance sheet to compete. AWS outspends on data centers, while most companies outspend on sales teams.

articles.title: FORWARD-DEPLOYED JOB TITLES
articles.summary: Palantir renamed its solutions engineers to "Forward-Deployed Engineers" and called them Deltas, like Delta Force. It sounded ridiculous but worked brilliantly. The role attracted high-agency, high-EQ engineers who might otherwise have seen client-facing work as beneath them. The title gave the work prestige, and Palantir got a hiring moat. Now, Harvey is doing the same thing with "legal engineer." The test for whether this is real or just title inflation: does the new title describe work that would have been unrecognizable five years ago? "Prompt engineer" already feels dated because the work turned out to be a feature of every job rather than a job itself.

articles.title: VERTICAL SAAS IS LOSING THE THING THAT MADE IT VERTICAL
articles.summary: Much of modern vertical SaaS has devolved into lightly rebranded horizontal software.

articles.title: AVERAGE DILUTION PER STAGE, IMPROVE NET IRR WITH CAPITAL RECYCLING
articles.summary: New research from AngelList on dilution benchmarks and how fund distributions actually work.
